---
title: "Preface"
---

# Experimental Physics: Methods, Measurement, and Apparatus {.unnumbered}

*A course and reference for advanced undergraduates, MSc, and PhD students.*

---

## Design philosophy {.unnumbered}

Most experimental-methods courses stop where the real work begins. This one is built in **two layers** so it stays useful from a third-year lab through a PhD:

- **Core** — the examinable, lecturable spine. Self-contained, follows a coherent narrative, assumes maths is fair game.
- **Bench Notes** — a parallel series of dense, single-topic reference cards in the spirit of JPE's *Precision Point* notes: one concept, the governing equations, a decision rule or table, and the failure modes. These are what a PhD student raids at 2 a.m. when the cryostat won't cool or the lock-in reads noise. Cross-referenced from the core, but they stand alone.

Four **threads** run through every module and are the real learning objectives:

1. **Uncertainty** — every number carries an error bar earned honestly.
2. **Signal vs. noise** — what limits you, and how to move the limit.
3. **Materials & mechanical design** — why apparatus behaves the way it does.
4. **Safety & craft** — the tacit knowledge that decides whether you keep your fingers and your weeks.

Everything is **reproducible**: every figure is generated by code in the repository from an included dataset. Every claim that can be cited, is. The depth ladder in each module --- **Core $\to$ Going deeper $\to$ Bench** --- lets the same text serve a BSc student and a postdoc.

**Prerequisites:** classical mechanics, E&M, basic quantum, calculus and linear algebra, and a working scripting language (Python assumed throughout).

---

## How to read each module {.unnumbered}

Each module is divided into tiers, marked by coloured callout blocks:

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Core
Examinable material; what every experimentalist must own. Suitable from advanced-undergraduate level.
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Going deeper
MSc/PhD extensions --- derivations, edge cases, and the regimes where the Core approximations break.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Bench
Pointers to the relevant [Bench Notes](bench-notes/index.qmd). The Bench Notes are the dense reference cards; these callouts are the signposts.
:::

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple" icon="false"}
## Key references
The canon for this topic. You should own these, or at least know where to find them.
:::

---

## Course outline {.unnumbered}

### Part I --- Measurement & Uncertainty

| Module | Title |
|--------|-------|
| M1 | [Probability and statistics, from scratch](parts/01-uncertainty/01-probability-statistics.qmd) |
| M2 | [Error propagation and the covariance matrix](parts/01-uncertainty/02-error-propagation.qmd) |
| M3 | [Estimation theory: the deep version of "fitting"](parts/01-uncertainty/03-estimation-fisher.qmd) |
| M4 | [Model fitting and goodness of fit](parts/01-uncertainty/04-model-fitting.qmd) |
| M5 | [Systematic errors and the error budget](parts/01-uncertainty/05-systematic-errors.qmd) |
| M6 | [Bayesian methods, sampling, and avoiding self-deception](parts/01-uncertainty/06-bayesian-methods.qmd) |

### Part II --- Signals & Noise

| Module | Title |
|--------|-------|
| M7 | [The physics of noise](parts/02-signals-noise/07-noise.qmd) |
| M8 | [Fourier analysis, sampling, and spectral estimation](parts/02-signals-noise/08-fourier-sampling.qmd) |
| M9 | [Filtering, analog and digital](parts/02-signals-noise/09-filtering.qmd) |
| M10 | [Phase-sensitive detection](parts/02-signals-noise/10-phase-sensitive-detection.qmd) |
| M11 | [Data acquisition](parts/02-signals-noise/11-data-acquisition.qmd) |

### Part III --- Electronics

| Module | Title |
|--------|-------|
| M12 | [Real components](parts/03-electronics/12-real-components.qmd) |
| M13 | [Op-amps and analog design](parts/03-electronics/13-opamps-analog.qmd) |
| M14 | [Semiconductors and the band gap](parts/03-electronics/14-semiconductors.qmd) |
| M15 | [Grounding, shielding, and EMI](parts/03-electronics/15-grounding-shielding-emi.qmd) |
| M16 | [Feedback and control](parts/03-electronics/16-feedback-control.qmd) |
| M17 | [Detectors](parts/03-electronics/17-detectors.qmd) |

### Part IV --- Mechanical, Vacuum, Cryogenics, Optics

| Module | Title |
|--------|-------|
| M18 | [Precision mechanical design](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/18-precision-mechanical.qmd) |
| M19 | [Materials selection](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/19-materials-selection.qmd) |
| M20 | [Manufacturing and tolerances](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/20-manufacturing-tolerances.qmd) |
| M21 | [Vibration isolation](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/21-vibration-isolation.qmd) |
| M22 | [Vacuum technology](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/22-vacuum-technology.qmd) |
| M23 | [Cryogenics](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/23-cryogenics.qmd) |
| M24 | [Thermometry and temperature control](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/24-thermometry.qmd) |
| M25 | [Optics and lasers](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/25-optics-lasers.qmd) |
| M26 | [Magnetics](parts/04-mechanical-vacuum-cryo-optics/26-magnetics.qmd) |

### Part V --- Practice & Craft

| Module | Title |
|--------|-------|
| M27 | [Soldering and interconnect](parts/05-practice-craft/27-soldering-interconnect.qmd) |
| M28 | [Chemicals and materials compatibility](parts/05-practice-craft/28-chemicals-compatibility.qmd) |
| M29 | [Safety, comprehensively](parts/05-practice-craft/29-safety.qmd) |
| M30 | [Lab craft --- the meta-skills](parts/05-practice-craft/30-lab-craft.qmd) |

### Capstone case studies

| | Title |
|--|-------|
| CS-A | [Temperature-stabilised photodiode readout](capstones/cs-a-photodiode-readout.qmd) |
| CS-B | [Vibration-isolated cryogenic optical setup](capstones/cs-b-cryo-optical-setup.qmd) |
| CS-C | [Measuring a small DC quantity at the statistical limit](capstones/cs-c-dc-measurement.qmd) |

---

## Detailed syllabus {.unnumbered}

### Part I --- Measurement & Uncertainty {.unnumbered}

**M1. Probability and statistics, from scratch**

- **Core:** random variables, PDF/CDF, expectation, variance, moments; the binomial, Poisson, and Gaussian and *when each applies*; the central limit theorem and its limits; counting statistics.
- **Going deeper:** characteristic functions; heavy tails and why the CLT can lie to you; order statistics; extreme-value distributions for rare-event experiments.
- **Bench:** *Distribution chooser*.
- **Refs:** Cowan, *Statistical Data Analysis*; Barlow, *Statistics*.

**M2. Error propagation and the covariance matrix**

- **Core:** propagation of uncertainty; correlated errors; the full covariance matrix as the central object (not the simplified add-in-quadrature special case).
- **Going deeper:** the Jacobian/delta method; propagating through nonlinear and implicit functions; when linear propagation fails and you must sample.
- **Bench:** *Error-budget template*.
- **Refs:** Hughes & Hase, *Measurements and their Uncertainties*; the GUM.

**M3. Estimation theory: the deep version of "fitting"**

- **Core:** estimators, bias, consistency, efficiency; least squares as a special case of maximum likelihood; the likelihood function.
- **Going deeper:** Fisher information, the Cramer-Rao bound, and the score; sufficient statistics; profile likelihoods and nuisance parameters; what "the best you can possibly do" means and how to design an experiment to approach it.
- **Bench:** *Fisher information & CRB on one page*.
- **Refs:** Cowan; Kay, *Fundamentals of Statistical Signal Processing: Estimation Theory*.

**M4. Model fitting and goodness of fit**

- **Core:** $\chi^2$ and least squares; weighted fits; reduced $\chi^2$ and how to read it; residual analysis; correlation in fit parameters.
- **Going deeper:** total least squares / errors-in-variables; robust fitting (Huber, RANSAC); regularization; degeneracy and identifiability; model selection (AIC/BIC, likelihood-ratio test, Wilks' theorem).
- **Bench:** *Reading a fit: residuals,* $\chi^2_\text{red}$*, and covariance*.
- **Refs:** Press et al., *Numerical Recipes*; Bevington & Robinson.

**M5. Systematic errors and the error budget**

- **Core:** the difference between statistical and systematic; calibration; how systematics hide; building an itemised error budget.
- **Going deeper:** correlated systematics across measurements; pull distributions; combining measurements with shared systematics; allocating effort where the budget is dominated.
- **Bench:** *Systematic-hunting checklist*; *Error-budget template*.

**M6. Bayesian methods, sampling, and avoiding self-deception**

- **Core:** priors, posteriors, credible intervals; frequentist vs. Bayesian framing and when the distinction bites; the bootstrap; Monte Carlo.
- **Going deeper:** MCMC (Metropolis--Hastings, HMC, nested sampling) and convergence diagnostics; marginalization over nuisance parameters; blind analysis and pre-registration to defeat experimenter bias.
- **Bench:** *Blind-analysis playbook*.
- **Refs:** Sivia & Skilling, *Data Analysis: A Bayesian Tutorial*; Gelman et al., *Bayesian Data Analysis*.

---

### Part II --- Signals & Noise {.unnumbered}

**M7. The physics of noise**

- **Core:** thermal (Johnson--Nyquist) noise; shot noise; 1/$f$ (flicker) noise; generation--recombination; quantization noise; noise temperature and noise figure; adding noise sources.
- **Going deeper:** the fluctuation--dissipation theorem as the unifying statement; quantum noise and the standard quantum limit; squeezing; correlated/back-action noise; noise in amplifier chains (Friis).
- **Bench:** *Noise-source cheat sheet (formulae + spectra)*.
- **Refs:** Ott, *Noise Reduction Techniques in Electronic Systems*; Horowitz & Hill, *The Art of Electronics*.

**M8. Fourier analysis, sampling, and spectral estimation**

- **Core:** Fourier series/transform, the DFT/FFT; the Nyquist--Shannon theorem and aliasing; windowing and spectral leakage; power spectral density and units (the dreaded $\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$).
- **Going deeper:** Allan deviation and how to tell whether averaging longer helps or drift dominates; Welch's method; cross-spectra and coherence; wavelets.
- **Bench:** *Reading a PSD*; *Allan deviation interpretation*.

**M9. Filtering, analog and digital**

- **Core:** RC/LC filters; low/high/band-pass; the notch filter for mains hum; Bode plots; filter order and roll-off.
- **Going deeper:** Butterworth/Chebyshev/Bessel trade-offs; FIR vs. IIR; phase distortion and group delay; matched filtering as the optimal linear detector; Wiener and Kalman filtering.
- **Bench:** *Filter selection table*.

**M10. Phase-sensitive detection**

- **Core:** the lock-in amplifier --- modulation/demodulation, why moving your signal off DC escapes 1/$f$, reference phase, time constant vs. bandwidth.
- **Going deeper:** boxcar/gated integration; correlation and cross-correlation detection; dual-phase and harmonic detection; modulation schemes (frequency, polarization, spatial) and choosing one.
- **Bench:** *Lock-in setup checklist*.

**M11. Data acquisition**

- **Core:** ADCs and DACs; resolution, sampling rate, and effective number of bits; sample-and-hold; triggering; grounding of instruments.
- **Going deeper:** ADC architectures (SAR, sigma-delta, flash) and where each wins; dithering; timing jitter and its noise penalty; FPGA/real-time acquisition; timestamping and synchronization.
- **Bench:** *Choosing an ADC*.

---

### Part III --- Electronics {.unnumbered}

**M12. Real components**

- **Core:** R, L, C and how real ones differ from ideal (ESR, parasitic inductance, dielectric absorption, tempco); reading a datasheet.
- **Going deeper:** capacitor dielectric types and where each belongs; resistor noise and self-heating; transmission-line behavior of "wires."
- **Bench:** *Capacitor dielectric selector*; *Reading a datasheet*.

**M13. Op-amps and analog design**

- **Core:** the ideal op-amp and the golden rules; inverting/non-inverting/difference/integrator/transimpedance; input offset, bias current, slew rate, GBW; stability and compensation.
- **Going deeper:** noise modeling of an amplifier stage; the transimpedance amplifier for photodiodes done properly; instrumentation amps; chopper/auto-zero techniques.
- **Bench:** *Transimpedance amp design*; *Op-amp noise budget*.
- **Refs:** Horowitz & Hill.

**M14. Semiconductors and the band gap**

- **Core:** band structure intuition; the band gap in eV (Si $\approx$ 1.12 eV, Ge $\approx$ 0.66 eV, GaAs $\approx$ 1.42 eV at 300 K); doping; the diode and transistor as devices; temperature dependence of the gap.
- **Going deeper:** direct vs. indirect gaps; carrier statistics; dark current and its activation energy; wide-bandgap materials (SiC, GaN).
- **Bench:** *Band gaps and detector materials table*.

**M15. Grounding, shielding, and EMI**

- **Core:** ground loops and how to break them; single-point vs. multipoint grounds; shielding; the Faraday cage; cable characteristic impedance and termination; decoupling capacitors.
- **Going deeper:** common-mode vs. differential-mode interference; cable transfer impedance; guarding for high-impedance measurements; mains-hum forensics; near-field vs. far-field coupling.
- **Bench:** *Grounding topologies*; *Hunting 50/60 Hz hum*.
- **Refs:** Ott; Morrison, *Grounding and Shielding*.

**M16. Feedback and control**

- **Core:** open vs. closed loop; PID; loop gain, bandwidth, and the gain--stability trade-off; locking a setpoint.
- **Going deeper:** transfer functions and loop shaping; phase/gain margin; servo design for temperature, laser, and cavity locks; Pound--Drever--Hall locking; feed-forward; anti-windup; digital control and discretization effects.
- **Bench:** *PID tuning*; *Anatomy of a servo lock*.

**M17. Detectors**

- **Core:** photodiodes (responsivity, dark current, junction capacitance); PMTs; APDs; their characteristic noise; biasing and readout.
- **Going deeper:** single-photon detection (SPADs, SNSPDs, TES); NEP and detectivity $D^*$; bolometers and the cryogenic detector zoo; matching a detector to a measurement.
- **Bench:** *Detector selection by regime*.

---

### Part IV --- Mechanical, Vacuum, Cryogenics, Optics {.unnumbered}

**M18. Precision mechanical design**

- **Core:** exact-constraint (kinematic) design --- counting degrees of freedom and constraining each exactly once; kinematic mounts; the thermal center; stiffness and the stiffness--damping trade-off.
- **Going deeper:** over-constraint, its symptoms and cures; quasi-kinematic and elastic-averaging design; flexures and monolithic mechanisms; Abbe error; metrology loops; designing for sub-nm stability.
- **Bench:** *Exact-constraint design*; *The thermal center*; *Curing over-constraint*; *Flexure design primer*.
- **Refs:** Smith & Chetwynd; Slocum, *Precision Machine Design*; JPE *Precision Point* notes.

**M19. Materials selection**

- **Core:** the property axes that matter --- electrical/thermal conductivity, thermal expansion, stiffness/density, hardness, machinability, magnetic permeability, outgassing, and cost.
- **Going deeper:** Ashby-style selection with competing constraints; cryogenic property changes; residual resistivity ratio (RRR) for cryogenic copper; galling and cold-welding pairs to avoid.
- **Bench:** *Metals comparison table*; *Cryo contraction integrals*; *Galling-prone material pairs*.
- **Refs:** Ekin, *Experimental Techniques for Low-Temperature Measurements*.

**M20. Manufacturing and tolerances**

- **Core:** turning, milling, drilling, grinding, EDM, sheet/laser/water-jet, casting, 3D printing; achievable tolerances of each process; surface finish; what a drawing must say.
- **Going deeper:** GD&T; tolerance stack-up analysis; fits and limits for holes, pipes, and shafts; ball-bearing classes and preload; lithography resolution; designing parts that are cheap to make; talking to a machinist.
- **Bench:** *Tolerance-by-process table*; *Fits & limits for holes/shafts*; *Bearing selection*; *Lithography resolution ladder*.

**M21. Vibration isolation**

- **Core:** the harmonic oscillator transmissibility curve; resonance and the $\sqrt{2}$ crossover; passive isolation (air tables, elastomers); finding and killing a resonance.
- **Going deeper:** multi-stage and pendulum isolation; active isolation and feedback limits; seismic noise spectra; geophones and accelerometers; isolating a cryostat without shorting the vibration path through cryogen lines.
- **Bench:** *Vibration isolation rules of thumb*.

**M22. Vacuum technology**

- **Core:** pressure regimes (rough/high/UHV) and the relevant gas-flow regime; pump types --- rotary vane, scroll, diaphragm, turbomolecular, ion, cryo, getter, diffusion --- and where each belongs; gauges (Pirani, capacitance manometer, ion); seals (KF/ISO, CF with copper gaskets, O-rings).
- **Going deeper:** conductance and pumping-speed calculations; outgassing, bake-out, and virtual leaks; leak detection with a helium leak detector; UHV materials and cleaning; designing a pumping manifold.
- **Bench:** *Pump selector by pressure*; *Seal/flange selector*; *Leak-hunting procedure*.
- **Refs:** O'Hanlon, *A User's Guide to Vacuum Technology*.

**M23. Cryogenics**

- **Core:** why and how things get cold; cryogen properties (LN$_2$, LHe); bath, flow, and closed-cycle (pulse-tube) cryostats; thermal contact and heat-sinking; the cooling-power vs. temperature picture.
- **Going deeper:** the dilution refrigerator and sub-Kelvin stages; adiabatic demagnetization; thermal-radiation shielding; wiring a cryostat; superconducting transitions; cryogen safety (asphyxiation, cold burns, pressure build-up).
- **Bench:** *Cryostat wiring & heat-sinking*; *Thermal-conductivity integrals*; *Cryogen safety*.
- **Refs:** Pobell, *Matter and Methods at Low Temperatures*; Ekin; Ventura & Risegari, *The Art of Cryogenics*.

**M24. Thermometry and temperature control**

- **Core:** thermocouples, RTDs (Pt100), thermistors, diode sensors, and resistance thermometers; self-heating; calibration and 2/3/4-wire wiring.
- **Going deeper:** cryogenic thermometry (Cernox, RuO$_2$) and magnetic-field dependence; primary vs. secondary thermometry; thermal lag and time constants; tying the sensor into a PID loop.
- **Bench:** *Thermometer selection by temperature/field*.

**M25. Optics and lasers**

- **Core:** aligning a laser as a procedure, not luck (two-mirror walk, irises, beam height); Gaussian beams (waist, Rayleigh range, divergence); lenses and mode matching; kinematic optical mounts.
- **Going deeper:** coupling into single-mode fiber; interferometry (Michelson, Mach--Zehnder, Fabry--Perot) and fringe reading; polarization handling; cavity locking; beam-quality $M^2$; thermal lensing.
- **Bench:** *Two-mirror beam-walking*; *Fiber coupling*; *Laser safety classes*.
- **Refs:** Moore, Davis & Coplan, *Building Scientific Apparatus*.

**M26. Magnetics**

- **Core:** generating fields (Helmholtz coils, electromagnets, superconducting magnets); shielding with mu-metal; Hall probes and fluxgates; field uniformity.
- **Going deeper:** persistent-mode superconducting magnets and quench; shimming; eddy-current and remanence effects; nulling Earth's field; magnetic cleanliness for sensitive experiments.

---

### Part V --- Practice & Craft {.unnumbered}

**M27. Soldering and interconnect**

- **Core:** how a good joint forms (wetting, intermetallics); iron temperature and tip care; solder alloy types (Sn63/Pb37 eutectic, lead-free SAC, silver-bearing, indium for cryo/optics); flux types and cleaning.
- **Going deeper:** soldering to difficult substrates; cryogenic and vacuum-compatible joints; spot-welding and wire-bonding; crimp vs. solder reliability; connector selection.
- **Bench:** *Solder alloy selection table*; *Flux types and cleaning*.

**M28. Chemicals and materials compatibility**

- **Core:** solvent compatibility --- which plastics survive acetone and which dissolve or craze (acetone attacks ABS, polystyrene, polycarbonate, PMMA; PTFE, PE, PP, and PEEK are safe); cleaning agents and order of operations; adhesives and epoxies (Stycast for cryo/vacuum, the cure-cycle gotchas).
- **Going deeper:** outgassing and vacuum-compatible materials; cryogenic adhesive behavior and differential contraction; surface preparation; chemical safety and waste.
- **Bench:** *Acetone (and common solvents) vs. plastics table*; *Epoxy & adhesive selector*.

**M29. Safety, comprehensively**

- **Core:** a real risk-assessment mindset; electrical (mains, capacitor banks, the let-go current), laser (classes, eyewear OD, beam-block discipline), cryogenic (asphyxiation, cold burns, over-pressure), vacuum (implosion, stored energy), chemical, RF/microwave, and magnetic (projectiles, pacemakers) hazards.
- **Going deeper:** interlocks and fail-safe design; lockout/tagout; oxygen-deficiency monitoring; pressure-system regulations; building safety into apparatus rather than bolting it on.
- **Bench:** *Hazard quick-reference per domain*.

**M30. Lab craft --- the meta-skills**

- **Core:** the lab notebook that survives scrutiny; reproducible analysis (git + notebooks where code generates every figure); a systematic troubleshooting/debugging methodology; reading datasheets; sourcing and vendors; cost/time estimation.
- **Going deeper:** experiment design and the order of de-risking; calibration chains and SI traceability; data management and provenance; writing the methods section so others can reproduce you.
- **Bench:** *Troubleshooting decision tree*; *Notebook & provenance standard*.

---

### Capstone case studies {.unnumbered}

Each capstone integrates threads from multiple parts onto one real subsystem.

- **CS-A --- Temperature-stabilised photodiode readout.** Detector (M17) $\to$ transimpedance amp + noise budget (M13) $\to$ grounding/shielding (M15) $\to$ PID temperature lock (M16, M24) $\to$ noise spectrum, PSD, Allan deviation (M7--M8) $\to$ fit and error budget (M2--M5).
- **CS-B --- Vibration-isolated cryogenic optical setup.** Kinematic/exact-constraint design (M18) $\to$ materials and contraction (M19) $\to$ vacuum and cryo wiring (M22--M23) $\to$ vibration isolation (M21) $\to$ laser alignment and fiber coupling (M25) $\to$ measured stability vs. predicted budget.
- **CS-C --- Measuring a small DC quantity at the statistical limit.** Modulation + lock-in (M10) $\to$ estimator design at the Cramer-Rao bound (M3) $\to$ blind analysis (M6) $\to$ systematic budget (M5).

---

## Licensing {.unnumbered}

- **Prose and figures:** [CC BY-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/)
- **Code:** [MIT](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

See `LICENSE-CONTENT` and `LICENSE-CODE` in the repository root.

---

## Building this book locally {.unnumbered}

```bash
conda activate expphys
quarto render          # builds both HTML and PDF
```

See the [repository](https://github.com/rw588/ExperimentalTextSite) for full setup instructions.
